fuzzy_prd:
We need a lightweight, in-process search library that our application can drop in to let users find items from a modest catalog without standing up any external service. The library should handle the whole pipeline from raw text to ranked results: normalize the text (case folding, stripping punctuation, splitting on spaces), reduce words to a common root form so that plural and singular forms match each other, then store a reverse index keyed by those normalized terms. Documents to be indexed are plain in-memory objects; each document must carry a single integer identifier field and one or more text fields. Some fields should be explicitly excluded from indexing — words appearing only in those excluded fields must never be searchable. The search API should support an optional field-restriction so callers can say 'look only in the title field'. Searching with an empty restriction list should return nothing. The system should also return prefix-based suggestions so the UI can show autocomplete hints as the user types — suggestions come back ranked by relevance. Adding the same document twice should be ignored (no duplicates). Removing a non-existent document should be a safe no-op. Invalid documents — missing identifier, wrong identifier type, or more than one identifier field — should produce a clear, machine-readable error category rather than a crash. Make the text-processing steps individually testable and composable as a pipeline so we can reuse the same normalization logic for both indexing and querying, similar to how the ranking helpers are shared internally.

One extra detail on the identifier side since the team asked: the document identifier field must be annotated with @Id from com.haroldadmin.lucilla.annotations.Id, and it must be of type Int. If a document has no @Id field, that should emit 'error=missing_id'. If the @Id field exists but is not Int, that should emit 'error=invalid_id_type'. If there is more than one @Id field, that should emit 'error=multiple_ids'. Same intent as before: clear machine-readable errors, not a crash.

Also, for excluded fields, this is specifically driven by @Ignore from com.haroldadmin.lucilla.annotations.Ignore. Anything annotated that way is left out of indexing. In the Book test type, the 'publisher' field is @Ignore, so if someone searches for a publisher value they should get no results even when that document is otherwise indexed.

On the text processing, the default pipeline is: lowercase, then strip_punctuation, then tokenize, then Porter stem. strip_punctuation removes commas, periods, colons, semicolons, hyphens, quotes, apostrophes, !, ?, parentheses. tokenize means split on whitespace. Queries go through that exact same pipeline too, so matching stays consistent between indexing and search, and the match term returned in search results is the stemmed form.

And for ranking helpers, the ld() and tfIdf() functions live in com.haroldadmin.lucilla.core.rank package, and both search ranking and autocomplete scoring use them. The levenshtein 'op' in the adapter maps directly to ld().

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with op) -> stdout token/transform/search/edit-distance results

trigger_keywords: what is the search output format
what does each search entry look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each search action emits one line 'search=<csv>' where csv is comma-separated entries sorted by document identifier (ascending integer order). Each entry is '<documentId>:<matchTerm>' where matchTerm is the Porter-stemmed query term. An empty result is 'search=' (nothing after the equals sign). Autocomplete emits 'autocomplete=<csv>' with suggestions in relevance order.

trigger_keywords: what does select empty list return
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An explicitly empty 'select' list (select: []) returns no results ('search='). With no 'select' key, all indexed fields are searched. Selecting a field name that does not exist on the document or that is @Ignore also returns no results.

Full Lucilla in-memory text-search surface: tokenize, lowercase, strip_punctuation, stem, transform pipeline including empty pipelines and ordered steps, Levenshtein/edit distance including empty strings, indexing multiple texts, action sequences for add/remove/search/autocomplete, ranking/scoring, and deterministic output order.

Route by op values tokenize, lowercase, strip_punctuation, stem, pipeline, levenshtein, and index. Use public_test_cases as exact authority for output tokens, distances, indexed documents, search matches, autocomplete rankings, and normalized errors.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: reduce words to a common root form so that plural and singular forms match
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Porter stemming algorithm implemented as the 'stem' transform step. The stemmed form is what gets stored in the inverted index and also what appears as 'matchTerm' in search results. For example 'melville' stems to 'melvil'.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the project PRD and the golden implementation under haroldadmin__lucilla/golden/core/src/main/kotlin/ for the authoritative specification.

feature1_1_lowercase.json.description: Lower-case text. Accepts a single text or list of texts.

input.text: No Toto I don't access my emails during a race

cases.expected_output: no toto i don't access my emails during a race

texts: No Michael no! This is not right
Toto, it's called a motor race

cases.expected_output:
no michael no! this is not right
toto, it's called a motor race
sorry?
it's called car racing

feature1_2_strip_punctuation.json.description: Remove punctuation symbols from a single text value.

input.text: Michael, have you got a minute?

cases.expected_output: Michael have you got a minute

feature1_3_tokenize.json.description: Split a text value into tokens on whitespace.

feature1_4_stemming.json.description: Reduce a word to its stem using the Porter stemming algorithm.

feature2_1_empty_pipeline.json.description: Run text through a processing pipeline that has no steps configured.

feature2_2_pipeline_steps.json.description: Run text through a multi-step processing pipeline.

feature3_edit_distance.json.description: Compute the Levenshtein edit distance between two strings.

feature4_1_index_size.json.description: Build an in-memory index and observe how many documents it holds.

feature4_2_remove.json.description: Remove a document from the index; removing a non-existent doc is a no-op.

feature4_3_search.json.description: Search the index for documents matching a query.
